verdict_reasoning: SRC is a cytoplasmic Src-family tyrosine kinase. It is myristoylated at its N-terminus, directing it to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane where it functions as a peripheral membrane protein on the cytoplasmic face. SRC has no transmembrane domain, no extracellular domain, no GPI anchor, and no documented stable non-covalent partnership with an extracellular-domain-bearing surface receptor that would co-traffic it intact to the outer leaflet. While SRC is activated downstream of many surface receptors and regulates their signaling, the SRC protein body itself remains cytoplasmic/inner-leaflet-anchored. No cell-surface-directed therapeutic (antibody, ADC, CAR-T, etc.) targets SRC protein body on the cell surface; SRC inhibitors are designed as intracellular kinase inhibitors. SRC is not secreted, not lysosomal, not induced to surface under stress or activation, and not a component of a stable extracellular complex. Ruling out contextual buckets: (1) cell_state_induced—no documented surface display under any condition; (2) tissue_restricted_surface—not applicable; (3) lysosomal_exocytosis—SRC is not a resident lysosomal/endosomal protein; (4) dual_localization—no PM pool; (5) stable_surface_attachment—no secreted or wash-resistant anchored pool. The protein body is inaccessible from the extracellular face.
